event: Gideon chooses three hundred soldiers at the water
symbols: men drinking at the water — the divine test that separated the vigilant lappers from the kneelers
vast army reduced to few — God's principle of working through weakness to display His power
river — the waters of testing and selection, a threshold of obedience
dramatic sky — the divine presence overseeing the selection
description: A black-and-white engraving depicting a large crowd of soldiers gathered at the banks of a body of water. Many men kneel or bend down to drink from the water, while others stand watching. The scene stretches across a wide landscape under a dramatic sky with sweeping cloud formations. In the background, more figures extend into the distance. Text at the bottom identifies the scene as Gideon choosing his soldiers. The composition conveys the vast scale of the army being tested.
theological_description: In Judges 7, God instructed Gideon to reduce his army from thirty-two thousand to a mere three hundred, lest Israel boast that their own strength had saved them. The method of selection was a test at the water: those who lapped water from their hands, remaining alert, were chosen; those who knelt to drink were sent home (Judg 7:4-7). This episode is a paradigmatic Old Testament demonstration of the principle that God's power is made perfect in weakness (2 Cor 12:9). The reduction of the army ensures that the coming victory over the Midianites can be attributed only to divine intervention, not human military might. The Fathers read this as an allegory of spiritual vigilance: the lappers represent the watchful believer who keeps guard even while attending to bodily needs, while the kneelers represent those who lose spiritual alertness. The three hundred also prefigure the faithful remnant — the Church as the small but chosen instrument through which God accomplishes His purposes in history.

scripture_refs.reason: The direct account of Gideon's selection of three hundred soldiers by the water-drinking test
The subsequent victory of the three hundred over the Midianites with trumpets and jars
God's power made perfect in weakness, the theological principle enacted in Gideon's reduced army
Gideon's earlier testing of God with the fleece, part of the same narrative of divine guidance
Jonathan's declaration that the Lord can save by many or by few, echoing the Gideon principle
